content.problemStatement:
Chef wants to build a data structure that behaves like a **Least Recently Used (LRU) cache** to efficiently manage key–value pairs.

Chef has a **fixed cache capacity** and wants to store keys with their values such that **when the cache exceeds this capacity, the least recently used key is removed**. Chef also wants to retrieve values by keys and update or add new key–value pairs efficiently.

Both retrieval and insertion operations must run in **average O(1)O(1)O(1) time**.

Class DescriptionChef will implement a class named **LruCacheLruCacheLruCache**.

The class supports the following operations:



**getValue(cacheKey)getValue(cacheKey)getValue(cacheKey)** Returns the value associated with cacheKeycacheKeycacheKey if it exists in the cache; otherwise returns −1-1−1.




**putValue(cacheKey,cacheValue)putValue(cacheKey, cacheValue)putValue(cacheKey,cacheValue)** Inserts or updates the key–value pair in the cache.

If the cache is already full, the **least recently used key** is evicted before inserting the new pair.



Constraints
1≤T≤1051 \leq T \leq 10^51≤T≤105
1≤cacheCapacity≤30001 \leq cacheCapacity \leq 30001≤cacheCapacity≤3000
1≤Q≤2×1051 \leq Q \leq 2 \times 10^51≤Q≤2×105
0≤cacheKey≤1040 \leq cacheKey \leq 10^40≤cacheKey≤104
0≤cacheValue≤1050 \leq cacheValue \leq 10^50≤cacheValue≤105
The total number of getValuegetValuegetValue and putValueputValueputValue operations across all test cases does not exceed 2×1052 \times 10^52×105.
Input Format
The first line contains a single integer TTT — the number of test cases.
For each test case:

The first line contains an integer cacheCapacitycacheCapacitycacheCapacity — the capacity of Chef’s LRU cache.
The next line contains an integer QQQ — the number of operations.
Each of the next QQQ lines contains one of the following operations:

get cacheKeyget\ cacheKeyget cacheKey — retrieve the value associated with cacheKeycacheKeycacheKey.
put cacheKey cacheValueput\ cacheKey\ cacheValueput cacheKey cacheValue — insert or update the key–value pair.




Output Format
For each getgetget operation, print a single line containing:

the value associated with cacheKeycacheKeycacheKey if it exists, or
−1-1−1 if the key does not exist in the cache.


print None for putputput operation.
content.inputFormat: The first line contains a single integer T, the number of test cases. For each test case, the first line contains an integer cacheCapacity, the capacity of Chef’s LRU cache. The next line contains an integer Q, the number of operations. Each of the next Q lines contains one of the following operations: get cacheKey — retrieve the value associated with cacheKey. put cacheKey cacheValue — insert or update the key–value pair.
content.outputFormat: For each get operation, print a single line containing the value associated with cacheKey if it exists, or -1 if the key does not exist in the cache. For put operation, print None.

put 2 2
get 1

get 2
put 4 4

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
put(1, 1)
Cache: {1=1}
Output: None


put(2, 2)
Cache: {1=1, 2=2}
Output: None


get(1)
Returns 1 and marks key 1 as most recently used
Cache order: {2=2, 1=1}
Output: 1


put(3, 3)
Cache is full ? least recently used key is 2, so it is evicted
Cache: {1=1, 3=3}
Output: None


get(2)
Key 2 is not present in the cache
Output: -1


put(4, 4)
Cache is full ? least recently used key is 1, so it is evicted
Cache: {3=3, 4=4}
Output: None


get(1)
Key 1 is not present in the cache
Output: -1


get(3)
Returns 3 and marks key 3 as most recently used
Cache order: {4=4, 3=3}
Output: 3


get(4)
Returns 4
Output: 4

put(1, 1)
Cache: {1=1}
Output: None


get(1)
Returns 1 and marks key 1 as most recently used
Cache order: {1=1}
Output: 1


put(2, 2)
Cache is full ? least recently used key is 1, so it is evicted
Cache: {2=2}
Output: None


get(1)
Key 1 is not present in the cache
Output: -1


get(2)
Returns 2
Output: -1

put(1, 1)
Cache: {1=1}
Output: None

Cache: {1=2}
Output: None

Cache order: {1=2}
Output: 2

Cache: {1=2, 2=3}
Output: None


get(2)
Returns 3 and marks key 2 as most recently used
Cache order: {1=2, 2=3}
Output: 3

solution.approach: To solve this problem, we can use a combination of a hash map and a doubly linked list to implement the LRU cache. The hash map will store the keys and their corresponding values, as well as a pointer to the node in the linked list. The linked list will be used to keep track of the order of the elements, with the most recently used element at the front and the least recently used element at the back. When a get operation is performed, we can check if the key exists in the hash map and if it does, we can move the corresponding node to the front of the linked list. When a put operation is performed, we can check if the key already exists in the hash map. If it does, we can update the value and move the corresponding node to the front of the linked list. If it does not, we can add a new node to the front of the linked list and update the hash map. If the cache is full, we can remove the least recently used element from the back of the linked list and the hash map.
solution.timeComplexity: O(1) per operation, O(Q) total. The time complexity is O(1) per operation because we are using a hash map and a linked list, which allow us to perform operations in constant time. The total time complexity is O(Q) because we are performing Q operations.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(cacheCapacity) — The space complexity is O(cacheCapacity) because we are storing at most cacheCapacity elements in the hash map and the linked list.
